perturbation.original_text: “Affiliate” means, with respect to party, any individual, Partner, corporation, partnership or other entity, directly or indirectly, controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, such party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Affiliate” means, with respect to party, any individual, Partner, corporation, partnership or other entity, directly or indirectly, controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, such party. For the purpose of this agreement, an affiliate excludes any entity where the controlling party owns less than 75% of the entity.
perturbation.explanation: The added text modifies the standard definition of 'affiliate' by introducing an ownership threshold, which may conflict with statutory definitions used for determining control and related-party transactions.
perturbation.law_explanation: The SEC definition of 'control' does not specify a minimum ownership percentage like 75%. The regulation defines control as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The modified contract text contradicts this by creating a bright-line ownership test that could exclude entities that are, in fact, under the control of the parent company.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the standard legal definition of 'affiliate' by adding an ownership threshold of 75%. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that the SEC definition of 'control' doesn't specify a minimum ownership percentage, defining it instead as 'the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' The scraped snippets, while not directly defining 'affiliate', points to 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions, suggesting that definitions within SEC regulations are relevant. By introducing a specific ownership percentage, the modified contract could exclude entities that are in reality controlled by the parent company but don't meet the 75% threshold, conflicting with the broader SEC definition of control.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means all financial, business and other information, in whatever form or medium (whether written, oral or electronic), including, without limitation, any trade secrets, processes, financial data, technical data and documentation, strategic planning, product/service specifications, prototypes, computer programs, drawings, models, customer information and marketing data that is furnished or disclosed by a Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely in connection with the Evaluation, except that such term will not include (i) information already known by the Receiving Party without an obligation of confidentiality, (ii) information that is or becomes publicly known other than through a breach by the Receiving Party of any of its obligations under this Agreement, (iii) information received by the Receiving Party from a third party who is not known by the Receiving Party to be under an obligation of confidence to the Disclosing Party, (iv) information independently developed by the Receiving Party without reliance upon the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and (v) information that the Disclosing Party has disclosed to other parties without similar obligations of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means all financial, business and other information, in whatever form or medium (whether written, oral or electronic), including, without limitation, any trade secrets, processes, financial data, technical data and documentation, strategic planning, product/service specifications, prototypes, computer programs, drawings, models, customer information and marketing data that is furnished or disclosed by a Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely in connection with the Evaluation, except that such term will not include (i) information already known by the Receiving Party, (ii) information that is or becomes publicly known, (iii) information received by the Receiving Party from a third party, (iv) information independently developed by the Receiving Party and (v) information that the Disclosing Party has disclosed to other parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrases 'without an obligation of confidentiality,' 'other than through a breach by the Receiving Party of any of its obligations under this Agreement,' and 'who is not known by the Receiving Party to be under an obligation of confidence to the Disclosing Party', the definition of Confidential Information is broadened. This could contradict trade secret law, which protects information that is kept secret and provides a competitive edge.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that (i) derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use, and (ii) is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Removing the clauses related to confidentiality obligations and breaches thereof weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially removing it from trade secret protection because the company is no longer reasonably protecting it.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens trade secret protection by removing clauses that emphasize the importance of maintaining confidentiality and preventing unauthorized disclosure. The original text explicitly excludes information already known, publicly available due to a breach, or received from a third party under no obligation. By removing these clauses, the changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information,' potentially including information that would not qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA, as described in the Law Explanation. This contradicts the efforts to maintain secrecy, which is a key element for trade secret protection. While the scraped law snippets are unhelpful here, the Law Explanation clearly establishes how the changed text creates a contradiction based on the UTSA. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction', because the re-defined term 'Confidential Information' no longer aligns with the trade secret laws.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will instruct its Representatives who have access to the Confidential Information to keep the same confidential by using the same care and discretion that the Receiving Party uses with respect to its own confidential property and trade secrets, which will be no less than reasonable care and discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will instruct its Representatives who have access to the Confidential Information to keep the same confidential by using such care and discretion as they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the 'reasonable care and discretion' standard with a subjective standard determined by the Representatives, weakening the obligation to protect confidential information. This could contradict the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition states that to maintain trade secret protection, the owner must take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of the information. Allowing Representatives to use their own discretion, without requiring a reasonable standard of care, is likely insufficient to maintain trade secret protection under this standard.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable care and discretion' to 'such care and discretion as they deem appropriate' weakens the standard of care for protecting confidential information. The Law Explanation states that the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition requires 'reasonable measures' to maintain trade secret protection. The modified text allows representatives to use their subjective discretion, which may fall below the 'reasonable measures' standard. This creates a legal contradiction by potentially failing to meet the legal requirement for maintaining trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts wholly made and performed in such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles, and excluding the Delaware Uniform Computer Information Transactions Act.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law from New York to Delaware and excluding the Delaware Uniform Computer Information Transactions Act (UCITA) introduces uncertainty if the contract involves software or digital information. UCITA is designed to govern software licenses and information transactions, and explicitly excluding it may lead to unintended consequences and conflict with established principles of contract law related to digital assets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Computer Information Transactions Act (UCITA) 6 Del. C. § 1301 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware UCITA governs computer information transactions and states that it applies to agreements with links to Delaware. By explicitly excluding UCITA, it could be argued that certain provisions, especially those dealing with software or information licensing integral to ADP's Marketplace, are not properly addressed, leading to legal disputes and a contradiction of Delaware's specific laws designed to handle such transactions. Even if the intent is to avoid specific UCITA provisions, a blanket exclusion creates ambiguity. This clause contradicts the intended scope and application of Delaware law related to computer information agreements.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract introduces a legal contradiction by explicitly excluding the Delaware Uniform Computer Information Transactions Act (UCITA). The Law Explanation states that UCITA governs computer information transactions and applies to agreements with links to Delaware. By excluding UCITA, the contract potentially contradicts Delaware's specific laws designed to handle transactions involving software or information licensing. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention UCITA, it does confirm that Title 6 of the Delaware Code addresses commerce and trade, providing a framework for commercial laws like UCITA. The perturbation explanation suggests that the exclusion of UCITA could lead to unintended consequences if the contract involves software or digital information, particularly ADP's Marketplace.
